annotations.label: Division by zero

anchor.snippet: division by zero , division where the divisor (denominator) is zero , is a problematic special case

annotations.note: Mathlib resolves division by zero by convention: `div_zero : a / 0 = 0` in any `GroupWithZero`.

annotations.label: Quotient in elementary arithmetic

anchor.snippet: The usual definition of the quotient in elementary arithmetic is the number which yields the dividend when multiplied by the divisor

annotations.note: Division as the inverse operation of multiplication is captured by `div_eq_mul_inv` (and unit-style `div_mul_cancel₀`) in the group-with-zero hierarchy.

annotations.label: Tending to infinity

anchor.snippet: the output of the function becomes arbitrarily large, and is said to " tend to infinity "

annotations.note: `Filter.Tendsto f l atTop` formalizes a function tending to infinity along a filter.

annotations.label: Indeterminate form

anchor.snippet: the expression is said to take an indeterminate form , as the resulting limit depends on the specific functions forming the fraction

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib defines `indeterminate form` as such; relevant instances are handled case-by-case (e.g. l'Hôpital).

annotations.label: Quotitive division

anchor.snippet: In quotitive division , the dividend

annotations.note: Mathlib has no separate definition of quotitive (measurement) division as a pedagogical concept.

annotations.label: Sandwiches from ten slices of bread

anchor.snippet: imagine ten slices of bread are to be made into sandwiches, each requiring two slices of bread

annotations.note: Pedagogical example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Division by zero via repeated subtraction never terminates

anchor.snippet: Because no finite number of subtractions of zero will ever exhaust a non-zero dividend, calculating division by zero in this way never terminates

annotations.note: No Mathlib theorem captures non-termination of an iterated subtraction algorithm at zero.

annotations.label: Partitive division

anchor.snippet: In partitive division , the dividend

annotations.note: Mathlib has no separate definition of partitive (sharing) division.

annotations.label: Ten cookies among friends

anchor.snippet: imagine ten cookies are to be divided among two friends

annotations.note: Pedagogical example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Division as ratio

anchor.snippet: In another interpretation, the quotient

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated `ratio` definition distinct from `HDiv.hDiv`.

annotations.label: Cake recipe ratio

anchor.snippet: a cake recipe might call for ten cups of flour and two cups of sugar

annotations.note: Pedagogical example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Slope of a line

anchor.snippet: The slope is defined to be the "rise" (change in vertical coordinate) divided by the "run" (change in horizontal coordinate) along the line

annotations.note: `slope f a b = (b - a)⁻¹ • (f b -ᵥ f a)` formalizes rise over run in an affine setting.

annotations.label: Division as inverse of multiplication

anchor.snippet: Division is the inverse of multiplication , meaning that multiplying and then dividing by the same non-zero quantity, or vice versa, leaves an original quantity unchanged

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `a / b = a * b⁻¹` via `div_eq_mul_inv`, with cancellation laws such as `mul_div_cancel_right₀`.

annotations.label: Six divided by three

anchor.snippet: a division problem such as "what is 6 divided by 3?" can be solved by rewriting it as an equivalent equation involving multiplication

annotations.note: Specific numeric example not stated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: No quotient for nonzero divided by zero

anchor.snippet: any number multiplied by zero is zero rather than six, so there exists no number which can substitute

annotations.note: Mathlib instead uses the junk convention `a / 0 = 0`; no theorem asserts non-existence of a quotient.

annotations.label: Zero divided by zero has no single quotient

anchor.snippet: any value can be substituted for the unknown quantity to yield a true statement, so there is no single number which can be assigned as the quotient

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `0 / 0 = 0` by convention; the statement of indeterminacy is not formalized.

annotations.label: Algebraic fallacy from division by zero

anchor.snippet: Using algebra , it is possible to disguise a division by zero

annotations.note: No Mathlib formalization of `1=2`-style fallacies arising from hidden division by zero.

annotations.label: Brahmagupta on division involving zero

anchor.snippet: A positive or negative number when divided by zero is a fraction with the zero as denominator

annotations.note: Historical attribution with no formal counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bhāskara II's infinite quantity

anchor.snippet: A quantity divided by zero becomes a fraction the denominator of which is zero. This fraction is termed an infinite quantity

annotations.note: Historical attribution with no formal counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Limit of a function

anchor.snippet: the value of the function

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Filter.Tendsto f l₁ l₂` is the filter-theoretic formalization of limits.

annotations.label: Vertical asymptote / tending to infinity

anchor.snippet: the function is said to " tend to infinity "

annotations.note: `tendsto_inv_nhdsGT_zero : Tendsto (·⁻¹) (𝓝[>] 0) atTop` formalizes the prototypical vertical asymptote at 0.

annotations.label: Reciprocal function as infinite singularity

anchor.snippet: A basic example of an infinite singularity is the reciprocal function

annotations.note: The reciprocal-at-zero singularity is exactly `tendsto_inv_nhdsGT_zero` (together with `tendsto_inv_atTop_nhdsGT_zero`).

annotations.label: Limit of a quotient equals quotient of limits

anchor.snippet: In most cases, the limit of a quotient of functions is equal to the quotient of the limits of each function separately

annotations.note: `Filter.Tendsto.div` shows `Tendsto (f/g) l (𝓝 (a/b))` given `Tendsto f l (𝓝 a)`, `Tendsto g l (𝓝 b)`, `b ≠ 0`.

annotations.label: Indeterminate form 0/0

anchor.snippet: the limit of the result cannot be determined from the separate limits, so is said to take an indeterminate form

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define `indeterminate form 0/0`; related techniques such as l'Hôpital's rule are formalized separately.

annotations.label: Affinely extended real numbers

anchor.snippet: The affinely extended real numbers are obtained from the real numbers

annotations.note: `EReal := WithBot (WithTop ℝ)` is the affinely extended real line.

annotations.label: Projectively extended real line

anchor.snippet: is the projectively extended real line , which is a one-point compactification of the real line

annotations.note: Mathlib has the generic `OnePoint` compactification and `OnePoint.equivProjectivization` for `OnePoint K ≃ ℙ K (Fin 2 → K)`, but no dedicated arithmetic on `OnePoint ℝ`.

annotations.label: Extended complex numbers / Riemann sphere

anchor.snippet: the extended complex numbers

annotations.note: `OnePoint ℂ` exists as a one-point compactification, but a dedicated `RiemannSphere` with full ring-of-meromorphic-arithmetic is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Arithmetic rules on the Riemann sphere

anchor.snippet: In the extended complex numbers, for any nonzero complex number

annotations.note: Mathlib does not equip `OnePoint ℂ` with the extended arithmetic rules (`a/0 = ∞`, etc.).

annotations.label: Construction of rational numbers

anchor.snippet: Starting with the set of ordered pairs of integers

annotations.note: `Rat.isFractionRing : IsFractionRing ℤ ℚ` together with the core `Rat` type formalize the construction as equivalence classes of integer pairs.

annotations.label: Division by zero in hyperreals and surreals

anchor.snippet: In the hyperreal numbers , division by zero is still impossible, but division by non-zero infinitesimals is possible

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Hyperreal` and `Surreal`; both inherit the `a/0 = 0` convention rather than asserting impossibility, but division by nonzero infinitesimals is supported via their field structure.

annotations.label: Extension of 1/x as a distribution

anchor.snippet: In distribution theory one can extend the function

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib defines a principal-value distribution extension of `1/x`.

annotations.label: Matrix pseudo-division

anchor.snippet: One can define a pseudo-division, by setting

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Matrix.inv` for invertible matrices but no Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse or pseudo-division.

annotations.label: Localization at zero is the trivial ring

anchor.snippet: the localization of every commutative ring at zero is the trivial ring

annotations.note: `IsLocalization.subsingleton (h : 0 ∈ M) : Subsingleton S` shows the localization is the zero ring whenever `0` is in the submonoid.

annotations.label: Wheel

anchor.snippet: any number system that forms a commutative ring can be extended to a structure called a wheel in which division by zero is always possible

annotations.note: No `Wheel` algebraic structure is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Division by a zero divisor in Z/6

anchor.snippet: For example, the ring

annotations.note: Mathlib has `ZMod 6` and zero-divisor predicates but no specific worked example of failed division by `2` or `3` in `ZMod 6`.

annotations.label: Division in a field via multiplicative inverse

anchor.snippet: In field theory, the expression

annotations.note: In a field/group-with-zero, `div_eq_mul_inv : a / b = a * b⁻¹` is exactly the field-theoretic definition.

annotations.label: IEEE 754 division by zero

anchor.snippet: In IEEE arithmetic, division of 0/0 or ∞/∞ results in NaN

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Data.FP.Basic` is an experimental floating-point sketch; IEEE 754 NaN/infinity semantics for `0/0` are not formalized.

annotations.label: Single-precision underflow producing infinity

anchor.snippet: For example, using single-precision IEEE arithmetic

annotations.note: No Mathlib formalization of single-precision underflow producing infinity.

annotations.label: Proof assistants define 1/0 = 0

anchor.snippet: Many proof assistants , such as Rocq and Lean , define 1/0 = 0

annotations.note: Mathlib's `div_zero : a / 0 = 0` (built on `inv_zero : (0 : G₀)⁻¹ = 0`) is exactly this convention.

annotations.label: USS Yorktown division by zero incident

anchor.snippet: a division by zero error in the "Remote Data Base Manager" aboard USS Yorktown

annotations.note: Historical anecdote with no Mathlib counterpart.
